title: A Japanese hanko / master craftsman's stamp aesthetic — the P letterform pressed in pure ink-black on warm white. The P stands for the product name; the precise cut across the bowl is the structural rule: bisecting the interior at the exact height of the contract. Precept's rules are inscribed into the definition at compile time; this mark captures that permanent inscription. No dark background. No glow. Maximum typographic contrast.
conceptualBridge: The ink-stamped P with rule cut means Precept's governing law is inscribed into the product's identity because the letterform cannot exist structurally without the cut that defines its interior boundary.
decision: Kept: 4.25 avg — the white-background hanko aesthetic is a genuine radical departure; the P letterform family is already well represented (04, 06, 09) so this stays exploring unless the aesthetic alone earns distinction over the existing P variants.
